title: Matsyendranātha: Akulavīratantra
author: attributed to Matsyendranātha (traditional)
tradition: Hindu; Kaula/Yoginī Kaula Śaiva-Śākta tantra (later associated with the Nātha Sampradāya)
genre: tantra
date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (the early Kaula stratum attributed to Matsyendranātha; manuscript redaction later)
summary: A short Kaula tantric scripture of the Yoginī Kaula stream attributed to the legendary master Matsyendranātha. It teaches a radically non-dual, antinomian path in which liberation comes through direct realization of the 'akula' (the transcendent reality beyond the kula/clan ordering of reality), declaring that the Akulavīra alone leads to liberation and that this truth cannot be confined by scriptures or philosophical schools.
history: Transmitted within the corpus associated with Matsyendranātha, founder-figure of the Yoginī Kaula and revered ancestor of the Nātha tradition; it was edited together with the Kaulajñānanirṇaya and related texts by P. C. Bagchi in the 1930s. Its attribution is traditional rather than securely historical, and it reflects the early medieval Kaula milieu honored by Abhinavagupta.

title: Ādyanātha: Anuttaraprakāśapañcāśikā
author: attributed to Ādyanātha (Ādinātha, the primordial Śiva-teacher; traditional/legendary attribution)
genre: philosophical stotra / didactic verse treatise (pañcāśikā, 'fifty verses')
date_estimate: uncertain; likely 1st-2nd millennium CE, a non-dual Śaiva devotional-philosophical poem
summary: A set of about fifty verses (pañcāśikā) expounding 'anuttara', the supreme unsurpassed reality of non-dual Śaivism, identified with pure consciousness, light (prakāśa) and the supreme 'I' (aham). The work is a condensed meditative-philosophical hymn on the recognition of this absolute as one's own nature.
history: Listed in Aufrecht's Catalogus Catalogorum as a Śaiva work. The ascribed author 'Ādyanātha/Ādinātha' is the mythic primordial guru (Śiva himself as first teacher), so the historical authorship and date are not securely established. It belongs to the broader non-dual Śaiva 'anuttara' literature exemplified by Abhinavagupta's Anuttarāṣṭikā.

title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Bhogakārikā
author: Sadyojyoti(s)
genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
date_estimate: c. 675-725 CE (Sadyojyoti, the first systematizing theologian of Śaiva Siddhānta)
summary: A versified treatise on 'bhoga', the soul's experience and enjoyment/suffering in bondage, analyzing how the impure self undergoes embodied experience and the karmic-material conditions of the bound soul (paśu). It belongs to the dualist metaphysics of Śaiva Siddhānta, in which Śiva (pati), souls (paśu) and bonds (pāśa) are eternally distinct realities.
history: Sadyojyoti is the earliest systematic theologian of the Śaiva Siddhānta whose works survive; the Bhogakārikā is transmitted as one of the eight foundational treatises (Aṣṭaprakaraṇa) and carries a commentary by Aghoraśiva (12th c.). It was edited and studied by W. A. Borody and others.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Mokṣakārikā
author: Sadyojyoti(s)
genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
date_estimate: c. 675-725 CE (Sadyojyoti)
summary: A verse treatise on 'mokṣa', liberation, expounding the Śaiva Siddhānta doctrine of how the bound soul is freed from the three bonds (āṇava, karma, māyā) through Śiva's grace and initiation, attaining a state of equality with Śiva (śivasāmya) without merging identically into him. It defends a dualist conception of liberation against rival non-dual views.
history: One of the surviving doctrinal kārikā-works of Sadyojyoti, transmitted within the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa with the standard commentary of Aghoraśiva. It is a core source for classical dualist Śaiva soteriology.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

title: Rāmakaṇṭha: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Nādakārikā
author: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha (Rāmakaṇṭha II)
tradition: Hindu; Śaiva Siddhānta (dualist Śaivism), Kashmirian lineage
genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
date_estimate: c. 950-1000 CE (Rāmakaṇṭha II, Kashmirian Śaiva Siddhānta lineage)
summary: A short verse treatise developing a Śaiva Siddhānta theory of 'nāda' (primal sound/resonance) and the metaphysics of sound, speech and meaning, treating the levels of phonic emanation that link the supreme principle to articulate language. It situates the doctrine of sound within the dualist cosmology and theology of the school.
history: Attributed in the tradition to Rāmakaṇṭha (the Kashmirian Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II, son of Nārāyaṇakaṇṭha), and transmitted within the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa with Aghoraśiva's commentary. Rāmakaṇṭha II is among the major 10th-century systematizers of dualist Śaivism.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Paramokṣanirāsakārikā
author: Sadyojyoti(s)
genre: polemical/philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
date_estimate: c. 675-725 CE (Sadyojyoti)
summary: A polemical verse treatise that refutes rival (para) conceptions of liberation (mokṣa), surveying and rejecting the soteriologies of other schools (such as those that posit liberation as annihilation, as merger, or as a quality-bearing state) in order to establish the Śaiva Siddhānta view. It is the school's classic doxographic critique of competing theories of release.
history: One of Sadyojyoti's authentic doctrinal works, transmitted in the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa; it received a commentary by Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II and is well known through modern critical editions and Alex Watson's studies of Śaiva refutations of rival mokṣa theories.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

title: Śrīkaṇṭha: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Ratnatrayaparīkṣā
author: Śrīkaṇṭha
tradition: Hindu; Śaiva Siddhānta (dualist Śaivism), Kashmirian lineage
genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
date_estimate: c. 10th c. CE (Śrīkaṇṭha, Kashmirian Śaiva Siddhānta lineage)
summary: An 'examination of the three gems' (ratnatraya), i.e. the three foundational categories of Śaiva Siddhānta — pati (the Lord/Śiva), paśu (the bound soul) and pāśa (bonds) — establishing their natures and mutual relations within the dualist system. It is a compact doctrinal analysis of the school's core triadic metaphysics.
history: Attributed to Śrīkaṇṭha, one of the 10th-century Kashmirian Śaiva Siddhānta authors; transmitted within the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa with the commentary of Aghoraśiva. The title and its threefold scheme were influential in later Tamil Śaiva Siddhānta theology.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

title: Bhojadeva: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Tattvaprakāśa
author: Bhojadeva (King Bhoja of Dhārā, Paramāra dynasty)
genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
date_estimate: c. 1018-1060 CE (reign of King Bhoja, early 11th c.)
summary: The 'illumination of the principles', a concise and lucid metaphysical compendium that condenses the vast scriptural corpus of Śaiva Siddhānta into a systematic exposition of its tattvas (reality-principles) and the pati-paśu-pāśa scheme. Traditionally the first text studied in the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa, it serves as the standard introduction to dualist Śaiva metaphysics.
history: Attributed to the famed polymath-king Bhoja of Dhārā, who is said to have distilled the Siddhānta scriptures into this single treatise; it is transmitted within the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa with the commentary of Aghoraśiva and remained a foundational teaching text in both Sanskritic and Tamil Śaiva Siddhānta.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Tattvasaṃgraha
author: Sadyojyoti(s)
genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
date_estimate: c. 675-725 CE (Sadyojyoti)
summary: A 'compendium of the principles', a brief verse summary of the tattvas (reality-categories) of Śaiva Siddhānta, setting out the constituents of the cosmos and the bound soul's situation within the dualist hierarchy of pati, paśu and pāśa. It distills the school's ontology into a teaching-friendly outline.
history: Counted among Sadyojyoti's authentic works and transmitted in the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa with Aghoraśiva's commentary. (Not to be confused with the Buddhist Tattvasaṃgraha of Śāntarakṣita, an entirely different text.)
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

title: Sadyojyoti: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa — Tattvatrayanirṇaya
author: Sadyojyoti(s)
genre: philosophical treatise (kārikā verses), one of the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa
date_estimate: c. 675-725 CE (Sadyojyoti)
summary: A 'determination of the three principles', a short treatise establishing the three eternal realities of Śaiva Siddhānta — pati (Śiva), paśu (the soul) and pāśa (bonds) — and their definitions. It is one of the earliest systematic statements of the school's signature triadic dualist ontology.
history: An authentic work of Sadyojyoti, transmitted within the Aṣṭaprakaraṇa and provided with a commentary (vṛtti) by Aghoraśiva; it is frequently cited in studies of early dualist Śaiva metaphysics.
related_works: Aṣṭaprakaraṇa (collection of eight Śaiva Siddhānta treatises)

title: Kṛṣṇānanda Āgamavāgīśa: Bṛhattantrasāra
author: Kṛṣṇānanda Āgamavāgīśa Bhaṭṭācārya
genre: tantric ritual digest/compendium (nibandha)
date_estimate: late 16th c. CE (Kṛṣṇānanda active c. 1575 CE in Bengal)
summary: A massive and authoritative compendium ('great essence of the tantras') that systematizes Śākta-tantric ritual practice — mantras, deity worship (including Kālī pūjā), yantras, initiation and other rites — drawing on some 170 tantric scriptures. It became the standard practical handbook of Bengali tantric Brahmanical worship and is still widely used today.
history: Composed in late-16th-century Bengal by Kṛṣṇānanda Āgamavāgīśa; the work was central to the consolidation of tantric orthodoxy in early-modern Bengal and is traditionally credited with shaping the iconography and ritual of Kālī worship there.
related_works: the c. 170 tantric scriptures it compiles

title: Śrīvatsa: Cidgaganacandrikā
author: Śrīvatsa (also called Kālidāsa)
tradition: Hindu; non-dual Śākta-Śaiva, Krama (Mahānaya/Kālīkrama) system of Kashmir Shaivism
genre: philosophical stotra / commentary (in praise of Kālī)
date_estimate: c. 13th-14th c. CE (disputed; sometimes placed earlier)
summary: A devotional-philosophical poem of 312 verses in four sections (vimarśa), praising Kālī as the absolute consciousness and expounding the Krama doctrine of the goddess as the dynamic 'moonlight in the sky of consciousness' (cid-gagana-candrikā). It treats macrocosmic and microcosmic emanation and the Śākta path (śāktopāya) to liberation, framed as a commentary/Pañcikā on Siddhanātha's Kramastuti.
history: Composed by Śrīvatsa, a pupil in the lineage at Pūrṇapīṭha, as an expansion of the Krama hymn tradition; it is among the most important poetic-theological works of the Krama school and survives with commentaries such as the Divyacakorikā and Kramaprakāśikā.

title: Raghunandanaprasāda Śukla: Dīkṣāprakāśa
author: Raghunandanaprasāda Śukla
genre: tantric ritual manual (paddhati) on initiation (dīkṣā)
date_estimate: uncertain; likely early-modern or modern (the named compiler/editor is not securely datable)
summary: An 'illumination of initiation', a manual setting out the procedures and theology of tantric/Śaiva initiation (dīkṣā) — the rites, mantras and stages by which a teacher consecrates a disciple. The work is a practical liturgical handbook focused specifically on the initiatory ceremony.
history: The text is attributed to Raghunandanaprasāda Śukla, of whom little is securely known; the title 'Dīkṣāprakāśa' could not be confidently matched to a well-documented historical work, so identification and dating remain uncertain.

title: Īśānaśivagurudeva: Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati, Volume 1
author: Īśānaśivagurudeva (Īśānaśiva)
tradition: Hindu; Śaiva (self-described Śaivasiddhānta, syncretic 'Kerala Tantra' combining Śaiva, Vaiṣṇava and Śākta elements)
genre: tantric/āgamic ritual manual (paddhati)
date_estimate: c. 11th-12th c. CE (the work as a whole; cited by texts of the 15th-16th c.)
summary: Volume 1 of a large encyclopedic ritual manual (paddhati) of the Kerala Śaiva tradition, covering the foundations of Śaiva ritual — including initiation (dīkṣā), daily and occasional worship, mantra-śāstra and the consecration of images and temples. It presents itself as a Śaivasiddhānta work while integrating Vaiṣṇava and Śākta deities and observances.
history: Compiled by Īśānaśivagurudeva, the work is a key witness to the syncretic 'Kerala Tantra' and was edited in four parts (Tantra, Mantra, Kriyā, Yoga pādas) in the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series; it is regularly cited in studies of South Indian Śaiva ritual. The volume divisions are modern editorial sections of one continuous text.

title: Īśānaśivagurudeva: Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati, Volume 2
author: Īśānaśivagurudeva (Īśānaśiva)
genre: tantric/āgamic ritual manual (paddhati)
date_estimate: c. 11th-12th c. CE (the work as a whole)
summary: Volume 2 (an editorial division of the continuous Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati) continuing the detailed exposition of Śaiva ritual practice — mantra-śāstra, the worship of deities, fire-rituals and the procedures of consecration and worship in the Kerala Tantric idiom. It belongs to the same encyclopedic manual as Volumes 1 and 3.
history: Part of the multi-volume Trivandrum Sanskrit Series edition of Īśānaśivagurudeva's paddhati; the volume boundary is a modern editorial convenience rather than a distinct work. The text documents the syncretic ritual world of medieval Kerala Śaivism.

title: Īśānaśivagurudeva: Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati, Volume 3
author: Īśānaśivagurudeva (Īśānaśiva)
genre: tantric/āgamic ritual manual (paddhati)
date_estimate: c. 11th-12th c. CE (the work as a whole)
summary: Volume 3 (a modern editorial division of the Īśānaśivagurudevapaddhati) concluding the manual's coverage of Śaiva ritual and including its treatment of yoga and the more advanced/esoteric portions of the system, alongside the temple- and image-related rites characteristic of Kerala Tantra. It forms the final part of the single encyclopedic work.
history: The closing volume of the Trivandrum Sanskrit Series edition of Īśānaśivagurudeva's paddhati; like the other volumes its boundary is editorial. The whole remains a primary source for medieval South Indian Śaiva ritual and theology.

title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī
author: Abhinavagupta
genre: bhāṣya/vṛtti (philosophical commentary)
date_estimate: c. 1000-1015 CE (Abhinavagupta fl. c. 975-1025 CE)
summary: Abhinavagupta's 'shorter' commentary (often called the laghvī-vimarśinī) on Utpaladeva's Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, the foundational verse-work of the Pratyabhijñā ('Recognition') school. It expounds the doctrine that liberation consists in recognizing one's own self as identical with Īśvara/Śiva, the one absolute consciousness whose freedom (svātantrya) projects the entire universe, and develops the school's epistemology and metaphysics of self-awareness.
history: A central work of Pratyabhijñā philosophy, written by the polymath Abhinavagupta as a commentary on Utpaladeva (fl. c. 925-975); it survives with the sub-commentary Bhāskarī of Bhāskarakaṇṭha and was edited and translated by K. C. Pandey and K. A. Subramania Iyer. It is one of the most influential statements of non-dual Śaiva idealism.

title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvivṛtivimarśinī, Volume 1
author: Abhinavagupta
genre: bhāṣya/vṛtti (extended philosophical commentary)
date_estimate: c. 1015-1020 CE (Abhinavagupta fl. c. 975-1025 CE)
summary: Volume 1 of Abhinavagupta's 'longer' commentary (the bṛhatī-vimarśinī), which comments not on Utpaladeva's verses alone but on Utpaladeva's own extended auto-commentary (vivṛti) on the Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā. This monumental work is the fullest exposition of Pratyabhijñā epistemology and metaphysics, treating perception, inference, the structure of consciousness and the recognition of the self as Śiva in exhaustive detail.
history: Composed after the shorter Vimarśinī, this commentary is doubly precious because Utpaladeva's vivṛti is largely lost, so Abhinavagupta's text preserves much of it; recovery of fragments of the vivṛti has been a major recent area of indological research. The volume division is editorial (the standard KSTS edition is in three parts).

title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvivṛtivimarśinī, Volume 2
author: Abhinavagupta
genre: bhāṣya/vṛtti (extended philosophical commentary)
date_estimate: c. 1015-1020 CE (Abhinavagupta fl. c. 975-1025 CE)
summary: Volume 2 (an editorial division) of Abhinavagupta's longer commentary on Utpaladeva's vivṛti to the Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā, continuing the detailed analysis of the Pratyabhijñā system — typically covering the central chapters on knowledge (jñāna) and action (kriyā) as the two powers of the recognizing self. It forms part of the same continuous work as Volumes 1 and 3.
history: Part of the three-volume KSTS edition of Abhinavagupta's bṛhatī-vimarśinī; the volume boundary is editorial. The work is a primary source for the mature philosophy of non-dual Kashmir Shaivism.

title: Abhinavagupta: Īśvarapratyabhijñāvivṛtivimarśinī, Volume 3
author: Abhinavagupta
genre: bhāṣya/vṛtti (extended philosophical commentary)
date_estimate: c. 1015-1020 CE (Abhinavagupta fl. c. 975-1025 CE)
summary: Volume 3 (an editorial division) concluding Abhinavagupta's longer commentary on Utpaladeva's vivṛti, completing the exposition of the Pratyabhijñā 'Recognition' philosophy — typically including the final sections on the categories of reality (tattvas) and the practical/soteriological dimension of recognizing the self as Śiva. It is the closing part of the single monumental commentary.
history: The final volume of the KSTS three-part edition of the bṛhatī-vimarśinī; the division is editorial. Together with the shorter Vimarśinī it represents the philosophical summit of the Pratyabhijñā school and was deeply influential on later Indian non-dualist thought.
